coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion capacitors (LiCs) are commonly used as power sources for electric vehicles (EVs) due to the combined advantages of electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) comprising high energy density, high power density, and long lifetime. However, the performance of the LiCs is susceptible to temperature. Therefore, a robust thermal management system (TMS) is crucial for EVs to operate efficiently and safely. In this work, holistic active and passive TMSs are designed to control the maximum temperature of the cell. In this regard, an air-cooled TMS (ACTMS) and a compact liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS) are among the active cooling systems, where pure paraffin phase change material (PCM), PCM with added aluminum mesh grid foil (PCM-Al), PCM with an added heat sink (PCM-HS), and heat pipe cooling system (HPCS) are the investigated passive TMSs. Moreover, the experimental results are verified against numerical analysis using a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software, COMSOL Multiphysics. The most efficient active and passive cooling systems are then selected in the CFD simulations to make a robust hybrid TMS for a module of LiC cells. The results exhibit that the LCTMS has the best performance where the maximum temperature of the cell is controlled at 32.5 °C, which shows the reduction of 41.2% compared to the natural convection (NC) case study. The PCM-HS has the best performance among the passive cooling systems, decreasing 38.3% compared to the NC. Moreover, the hybrid TMS decreases the maximum temperature by 50.1% while uniformizing the temperature along with the module by keeping the temperature difference below 4.5 °C between the coldest and hottest points on the cell.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of holistic active and passive thermal management systems for electric vehicles: Fast charge and discharge applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion capacitors (LiCs) are commonly used as power sources for electric vehicles (EVs) due to the combined advantages of electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) comprising high energy density, high power density, and long lifetime. However, the performance of the LiCs is susceptible to temperature. Therefore, a robust thermal management system (TMS) is crucial for EVs to operate efficiently and safely. In this work, holistic active and passive TMSs are designed to control the maximum temperature of the cell. In this regard, an air-cooled TMS (ACTMS) and a compact liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS) are among the active cooling systems, where pure paraffin phase change material (PCM), PCM with added aluminum mesh grid foil (PCM-Al), PCM with an added heat sink (PCM-HS), and heat pipe cooling system (HPCS) are the investigated passive TMSs. Moreover, the experimental results are verified against numerical analysis using a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software, COMSOL Multiphysics. The most efficient active and passive cooling systems are then selected in the CFD simulations to make a robust hybrid TMS for a module of LiC cells. The results exhibit that the LCTMS has the best performance where the maximum temperature of the cell is controlled at 32.5 °C, which shows the reduction of 41.2% compared to the natural convection (NC) case study. The PCM-HS has the best performance among the passive cooling systems, decreasing 38.3% compared to the NC. Moreover, the hybrid TMS decreases the maximum temperature by 50.1% while uniformizing the temperature along with the module by keeping the temperature difference below 4.5 °C between the coldest and hottest points on the cell.

ce:section.ce:para: Global warming and vehicular emissions have led to government policies to develop renewable energy systems [1]. Emission reduction policies have led to the electrification of vehicles, in which electrical energy storage systems (ESS) are the main contributor to electric vehicles (EVs) [2]. Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) are among the most used ESSs in the market due to high energy densities [3]. However, LIBs suffer from low power densities, an obstacle to achieving high acceleration demands [4]. Furthermore, operating in urban driving cycles with high current rates for charging/discharging affects their lifetime and efficiency [5].

On the other hand, electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) show high power densities, but their energy density is relatively low [6]. Therefore, a good EES is required to deliver high energy and power densities with long cycle life [7]. In this regard, a hybrid ESS has emerged that consolidates LiBs negative electrode (Li-ion doped carbon) and EDLCs positive electrode (activated carbon) [8]. The hybrid ESS is known as the lithium-ion capacitor (LiC) that has the benefits of EDLCs and LiBs such as high-power densities up to 10 kW/kg, high energy density around 14 Wh/kg, and higher voltage level (maximum of 3.8 V) [9].

The most common phenomena during an electrochemical call's operation are heat generation [10]. Therefore, despite the favorable advantages, LiCs performance is susceptible to excessive heat generation when employed in high power applications [11,12]. High cell temperature has an undesirable impact that will drastically reduce the lifetime of LiCs [13]. Furthermore, the most critical concern in the LiC module/pack is temperature non-uniformity which results in electrical imbalance and causes the state of charge and voltage mismatch between LiC cells [14]. Hence, a robust thermal management system (TMS) is required to assure safe and reliable working of the LiCs in high power applications [15]. Moreover, the 3D thermal model development is crucial before TMS design to have a deep insight into the LiC behavior [16]. In this context, the 1D electro-thermal should be developed before designing the 3D thermal model, to extract the hat generation [17]. TMS and thermal modeling for LiC is not well documented in the literature, unfortunately. Therefore, in this work, validated active and passive TMSs are introduced and investigated experimentally and numerically. Generally, there are two common cooling methods, including active and passive systems [18]. The active cooling system counts for air cooling systems [19], liquid cooling systems [20,21], thermoelectric [22,23] and refrigerant cooling systems [24,25]. In contrast, the passive cooling system comprises phase change materials (PCM) [26,27], heat pipes [28,29], and heat sinks [30,31].

The most commonly used cooling technique in ESSs is the air cooling method which is simple, intensely adaptable, with low maintenance cost [32,33]. The cooling performance of this system relates to the space between each cell, the distance between fans and cells, the inlet and outlet position, the number of inlets and outlets, and initial and boundary conditions [19]. Soltani et al. [34] developed a three-dimensional thermal model for a LiC module employing the air-cooling method as the TMS. Then, the design parameters of the TMS were optimized, including inlet air velocity, spacing between cells, and the direction of the flow. They proved that the side cooling with 5 m/s air velocity and 5 mm space between cells is the optimized air-cooled topology. Zhang et al. [35] proposed a novel air-cooled method based on multi-vents and optimized the system by changing the position, size, and number of vents. It was seen that the maximum temperature and the temperature difference of the module reduced by 16.6% and 76%, respectively. The air-cooling system is not efficient in hot ambient temperatures and where the heat transfer coefficient is low.

Thanks to the high heat transfer coefficient of the liquid cooling method [36], this system is one of the most efficient active cooling systems [37,38]. Karimi et al. [39] investigated a compact and optimized liquid cooling system for a single LiC cell in which the maximum cell's temperature was decreased by 23.1 °C. Design considerations including inlet coolant flow rate, channel diameter, inlet coolant temperature, coolant path design, and the impact of gap filler were studied numerically. Tang et al. [40] analyzed a liquid cooling TMS for Evs based on machine learning. The thermal performance of the battery was estimated based on a support vector regression model. Xu et al. [41] optimized a liquid cooling system for a battery pack. The optimized scheme reduced the maximum temperature difference by 7.49% compared with the initial one. Tan et al. [42] investigated a direct liquid cooling numerically for a fast charging LiB pack in hydrofluoroether. The maximum temperature difference and standard deviation were reduced by 18.1% and 25%, respectively. Akbarzadeh et al. [43] proposed a novel liquid cold plate embedded with PCM that the new cold plate was able to reduce pump energy consumption and weight of the plate by 30% and 36%, respectively.

The PCM cooling technique is a passive system taking advantage of its high latent heat of fusion that absorbs thermal energy losses [44,45]. During the PCM's phase change, the specific heat would be increased when it starts to be melting from the solid state [46]. PCMs maintenance is not required because they do not have any moving parts. Moreover, there is no external energy source for PCMs to work. Paraffin is the most employed material as the PCM due to its valuable advantage of having a long lifetime since paraffin is chemically compatible with metals. However, PCMs suffer from low thermal conductivity that limits their performance to release the absorbed heat. In this regard, many studies have been conducted to increase the PCM's thermal conductivity by adding a secondary cooling system to PCMs such as Nanomaterials, aluminum mesh grid foil [47], copper mesh grid foil [48], heat sink [49], and heat pipe [50]. Jaguemont et al. [51] studied a paraffin PCM for a dual LiC cell module in which the maximum temperature was decreased by 6 °C.

On the other hand, Heat pipes are two-phase heat transfer devices that can effectively transfer heat [52]. Behi et al. [53] numerically studied the cooling effect of L-shaped heat pipes for a cylindrical battery module. They found that the heat pipe cooling system directly affects the temperature reduction and uniformity of the battery module. Behi et al. [54] experimented with the sandwich heat pipe-based battery TMS for fast discharging of prismatic LTO cells. They found that the proposed TMS can reduce the battery temperature by 33.4% compared to natural convection.

The most critical problem of battery modules is temperature uniformity that can be solved by a robust TMS [55]. In this regard, a hybrid system can minimize the temperature difference of the battery module [56]. Behi et al. [49] proposed a TMS for LTO battery based on heat pipe and liquid cooling system to identify the hot spots of the cell. The proposed system reduced the maximum temperature by 29.9% and 32.65 for the liquid-cooled and hybrid liquid heat pipe systems. In another research, he investigated a hybrid system in which the maximum temperature for the air cooling, heat pipe, and heat pipe with copper sheets were 42.4, 37.5, and 37.1 °C, respectively [53]. He proposes a sandwich configuration of heat pipe for high current rates. The temperature reduction of 13.7%, 31.6%, and 33.4% was observed for heat pipe with natural convection, heat pipe with forced convection, and heat pipe with the air-cooled system, respectively [57].

TMSs for the LiC technology are seldom reported in the literature to the best of the authors' knowledge. In this work, Holistic active and passive TMSs are designed to control the maximum temperature of the cell. In this regard, an air-cooled TMS (ACTMS) and a compact liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS) are among the investigated active cooling systems, where pure paraffin phase change material (PCM), PCM with added aluminum mesh grid foil (PCM-Al), PCM with an added heat sink (PCM-HS), and heat pipe cooling system (HPCS) are the investigated passive TMSs. Moreover, the experimental results are verified against numerical analysis using a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software, COMSOL Multiphysics. Then, the numerical simulations for single-cell are extended to a module of six cells to analyze the cooling performance of the proposed cooling methods for the module.

The present work aims to control the maximum temperature at the desired range and minimize the temperature uniformity meaning that the temperature difference between the coldest and hottest points on the cell should be less than 5 °C. In the final step, the most efficient active and passive cooling systems are selected in the CFD simulations to make a robust hybrid TMS for a module of LiCs. Therefore, a hybrid TMS comprising LCTMS and pure PCM is developed to study its cooling performance for the LiC module. Comparing such comprehensive active and passive systems has never been addressed in the literature for the LiC technology.

The present work investigates Lithium-ion capacitor (LiC) cell behavior under a 150 A fast charge/discharge cycling regime. Then, different active and passive thermal management systems (TMS) are proposed to investigate their performance to control the heat generation of the cell under such an intense current rate. In this regard, first, the specifications of the used LiC technology are introduced, and then, the test methodology is explained. Finally, a test bench for different proposed TMSs is described, and the experimental results are compared to study the effectiveness of the employed cooling methods.

The LiC is a hybrid energy storage system (ESS) that merges the advantages of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) and electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs). Therefore, the LiC technology has come into existence with the advantages of high power density, long cycle life, high charge and discharge capability, and high energy density. The used LiC cell is a prismatic commercial 2300 F ESS, that the specifications are listed in Table 1 .

The experimental tests are carried out at the Vrije Universiteit Brussel-Battery Innovation Center (BIC). The PEC battery cycler and the CTS climate chamber with 80 channels supporting 50 A and 5 V are used to analyze the cell's function at a controlled temperature during 150 A continuous charge/discharge current rate by measuring the cell response over 1400 s with 1 s time step. Fig. 1 shows the overall view of the battery tester and the climate chamber. The voltage reding precision of the battery cycler is ±0.005%. A remote computer is employed to record the current and voltage of the cell during the tests. The battery cycler is responsible for cycling the cell under predefined conditions. Each complete charging/discharging of the cell takes almost 36 s, and the cycling has no rest time between two charge/discharge regimes to investigate the cell's performance under a high duty application. One cycle of the voltage and current profile for the LiC cell is illustrated in Fig. 2 . In addition, K-type thermocouples, a data logger, and a computer are employed to monitor the temperature data. The precision of the data logger and the thermocouples are 0.025 °C and 0.2 °C, respectively.

Thanks to its high cooling performance, the active cooling method is one of the most promising cooling techniques. Generally, air cooling systems and liquid cooling systems are amongst the used active cooling methods for EVs. Both of the mentioned cooling methods are tested experimentally to compare the results and find the most optimal cooling system for high power applications.

The proposed air-cooled TMS (ACTMS) for the LiC cell consists of four DC axial 12 V fans. The dimensions of the used fans are 80 mm in length. 80 mm in width, and 25 mm in thickness. The cell is located in the middle of an air-cooling box, in which two fans are embedded on each side of the box. Two fans on the right side are responsible for blowing the air from the environment into the cooling box, and two other fans on the left side are responsible for air suction from the cooling box to the environment. The proposed ACTMS is shown in Fig. 9(a). In the ACTMS, a power supply controls the air mass flow rate, where the thermocouples and the data logger monitor the temperature evolution during the cycle.

The experimental test results for the ACTMS case study can be seen in Fig. 3 . When the LiC cell is working under a continuous 150 A charge/discharge driving cycle, it generates too much heat, resulting in lifetime reduction. In the worst scenario, a thermal runaway will happen. As is evident from the figure, the maximum temperature of the cell under natural convection when there is no cooling system reaches 55.3 °C after 1400 s, which is way beyond the safety operational range for such a technology. On the other hand, when the ACTMS is employed, the temperature is controlled at 37.8 °C. Therefore, the ACTMS strategy is robust enough to cool down the cell's temperature at the desired range.

The designed liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS) is another active cooling method in which two compact cold plates are connected on the right and left sides of the LiC cell through a thin layer of thermal grease as the gap filler. The dimensions of the cold plates are 170 mm in length, 100 mm in width, and 7 mm in thickness. Fig. 9(b) exhibits the LCTMS in which a power supply controls the pump speed. Besides, water as the coolant flows through the radiator and the pipes with 100 mL/min mass flow rate thanks to the pump and the water tank responsible for flowing the coolant and storing it, respectively. The employed pump is small on a lab-scale, which would not be suitable for EV applications.

Nevertheless, the thermal elements in actual size should be selected for real-life EV applications. The circulation pump is among the thermal elements vital to the liquid cooling system to flow the coolant from the tank to the pipes and radiator. Fig. 4 shows the temperature evolution of the LiC cell when LCTMS is used as the cooling system. The maximum cell temperature is controlled at 32.5 °C that shows the robustness of the LCTMS strategy. This system has better performance than the ACTMS, thanks to its high heat transfer coefficient.

The passive cooling method is getting more attention due to low energy consumption when operating alone or with other cooling systems. The typical cooling systems for passive cooling of EVs are phase change materials (PCM) and heat pipes.

The test bench system comprises the PCM and a polyvinyl chloride (PVC) container to keep the PCM inside the LiC cell. Pure paraffin PCM is used in this section as the TMS to study if such a system can maintain the maximum cell temperature at the desired limit. In this regard, the PCM is poured into the PVC container containing 1.3 cm of PCM around the cell. The schematic of the PCM TMS is demonstrated in Fig. 9(c).

Fig. 5 exhibits the temperature distribution of the LiC cell when the pure paraffin PCM is cooling it. Based on the figure, the paraffin PCM absorbs the generated heat during the cycling regime. However, the PCM cannot reject and release the absorbed heat because of its low thermal conductivity. The maximum temperature of the cell with the added PCM is 40.8 °C, which is a bit higher than the safe limit that the LiC cell's manufacturer announced (less than 40 °C). Therefore, another added system with high thermal conductivity, such as an aluminum mesh grid foil or an aluminum heat sink, should be added to the PCM TMS strategy to increase its thermal conductivity and release the heat from the PCM to the surrounding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aluminum foil grid mesh added to the PCM
ce:section.ce:para: The critical disadvantage of the PCM can be solved by adding an extra material with high thermal conductivity to the system. This section employs the aluminum foil grid mesh (Al-mesh) to enhance the PCM's low thermal conductivity. Therefore, three rounds of Al- mesh are screwed on the LiC cell to release the absorbed heat from the PCM to the environment. The schematic of the Al-mesh is shown in Fig. 9(d). The results of the added material to the PCM are seen in Fig. 6 , in which the maximum temperature of the cell is 36.2 °C. This trend proves a 4.6 °C decrease in the final temperature stemming from the added system's high heat transfer to the PCM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aluminum heat sink added to the PCM
ce:section.ce:para: The second high thermal conductive material added to the pure PCM is an aluminum heat sink (Al-HS), shown in Fig. 9(e). The cooling capacity would be enhanced using such a hybrid system due to the simultaneous employment of latent heat storage (PCM) and rejection of the absorbed heat (Al-HS). Therefore, two Al-HS are added to the right and left sides of the LiC cell using a thin layer of thermal grease as the gap filler. Most of the heat is stored in the pure PCM due to its high latent heat storage. Using the Al-HS helps the PCM to release the absorbed heat to the ambient. The PCM and the Al-HS potential are utilized simultaneously to cool down the LiC cell in such a hybrid PCM-HS design.

Fig. 7 proves that the hybrid PCM-HS system is better than the PCM-Al system to lower the cell's temperature. The PCM-HS system operates well in the early stage of the fast charge/discharge regime and keeps this high performance until the end of the experiment. The temperature trend of the cell during the test proves that the PCM-HS system has high performance during the melting time of the PCM.

The second passive cooling method is heat pipe cooling system (HPCS), in which the schematic of the system is seen in Fig. 9(f). In this section, the thermal performance of HPCS is studied to be compared with the PCM cooling system. To do so, six flat heat pipes are employed in which three heat pipes are embedded on two sides of the cell using a thin layer of thermal grease. The heat pipes are close to the tabs of the cell since the most heat loss zone is located near the tabs of the cell. The HPCS absorbs the heat from the cell through its evaporator section and releases it to the ambient, thanks to its condenser section. Fig. 8
illustrates that the thermal performance of the HPCS is good compared to the natural convection case study. After using the HPCS, the cell's temperature is around 48.9 °C showing that the cell is still not operating in the safe working range. The temperature curves for both HPCS and NC case studies show the same variation trend in which both curves rise and increase over time and reach almost a steady state at the end of the test. The temperature difference between the NC and the HPCS modes is around 6.4 °C at the end of the fast charge/discharge regime. This difference proves that the HPCS is a good cooling system for a limited temperature rise.

The thermal performance of the proposed TMSs is compared in this section, as shown in Fig. 10 , to investigate which of the abovementioned cooling systems is more robust to cool down the single LiC cell. All the initial conditions are the same for the proposed TMSs. The initial room temperature kept constant at 23 °C during the 1400 s test regime with 1 s time step. The summary of the thermal performance of the proposed TMS topologies is listed in Table 3
, in which it is apparent that the LCTMS performance is better than the rest of the cooling systems. Natural convection (NC) is the case study that all the other case studies are compared. Therefore, the performance of all the proposed cooling systems is evaluated based on NC performance to have a clearer view of the cooling performance of the proposed cooling systems. As listed in Table 3, the first rank among all the proposed cooling systems belongs to the LCTMS case study.

Compared with the NC case study, the maximum temperature, temperature difference, and temperature reduction of the LCTMS are 32.5 °C, 9.5 °C, and 41.2%. For the ACTMS, the temperature reduction is 31.6%, with a 14.8 °C temperature difference along the cell's surface. Comparing the performance of the pure PCM and HPCS shows that the pure PCM is better than the HPCS, but it is not a good solution for high current applications due to the problem of low thermal conductivity. Also, HPCS should be used as a secondary condenser to obtain its best efficiency. The HPCS is gravity-dependent with complex design, therefore using the HPCS alone would not be a good solution for high power applications. Among the passive cooling systems, the PCM-HS has the best performance. It controls the maximum temperature at 34.1 °C, temperature uniformity is 11.1 °C and temperature reduction compared to NC is 38.3%, which is quite good. Having a look at the PCM-Al and PCM-HS results proves that solving the thermal conductivity of the PCM will be a promising technique to remove the absorbed heat to the ambient.
Table 2 lists the test bench components and material properties used in the ACTMS, LCTMS, PCM, PCM-Al-mesh, PCM-HS, and HPCS systems.

Moreover, from the temperature uniformity point of view, the same conclusions can be made. The temperature uniformity for the NC case study is 32.33 °C that is too high, while the ACTMS and LCTMS as active cooling systems are 14.86 and 9.48 °C, respectively. This proves that active cooling systems can decrease the temperature uniformity up to 70% compared to the NC case study. For the passive cooling systems, the temperature uniformity of the PCM, PCM-Al, PCM-HS, and HPCS are 17.83, 13.25, 11.05, and 25.89 °C, respectively. The PCM-HS TMS is the best cooling system among the passive cooling systems. It can be concluded that enhancing the thermal conductivity of the PCM results in a significant improvement in its thermal performance, where the temperature uniformity of PCM-HS decreased by 38% compared to the pure PCM. Hence, the pure paraffin PCM should be mixed with another cooling method/material to exhibit its best thermal performance. Therefore, hybridizing the best active cooling system (LCTMS) with the pure PCM comes up, designed and investigated in the next section. Fig. 11 compares the temperature uniformity of the investigated cooling systems.

In this work, the electro-thermal parameters of the LiC cell are identified using MATLAB/SIMULINK® platform to calculate the system's heat loss. Following the 1D electro-thermal model, the 3D sketch of the model is designed employing Autodesk INVENTOR® software, and the CFD model is then analyzed using COMSOL Multiphysics®, thanks to its fluid-solid-thermal exceptional functions. In this regard, the governing equations for the LiC heat generation, fluid flow analysis, and PCM modeling is explained in detail in this section. The reason for including only liquid cooling and PCM equations in this section is due to the high performance of the liquid cooling system, and also since the PCM robustness increase by enhancing its thermal conductivity, a hybrid TMS including these two cooling methods will be developed in this section to see how robust would be the new hybrid TMS.

The target cell is the commercial prismatic 2300 F LiC cell. The heat is generated during the cell's operation, including the electrochemical reaction loss, the side reaction loss, and the Joule loss [58]. The side reaction loss is neglected because the cell is working in its safe temperature limit [59]. In this work, the cell's heat sources have been divided into tab and cell domains. The generated heat for the cell domain includes reversible and irreversible heat that comes from the internal resistance and entropy change [60]:

The method of modeling the PCM in this work is the effective heat capacity method (EHC) because the phase transition occurs within a range of different temperatures. In the mentioned method, the equation for the solid phase is the same as the liquid phase regarding the adjustment of enthalpy [68]. The main factor of the phase transition region, also called the mushy zone, is the EHC value. The EHC value is maximum at phase change temperature by selecting the melting bounds. Considering the average of the solid and liquid phases for the mushy zone, each phase's thermal conductivity and density would differ [69].

amb is considered at the beginning of the numerical simulations where t = 0. The environment temperature of 23 °C is the initial condition for all the simulation studies in this work. The simulation time is set to 1400 s with a step time of 1 s. The fluid flow rate is 100 mL/min (two cooling plates for a single LiC cell), the PCM mass is 0.33 kg, and the PCM thickness is 13 mm. Moreover, the radiation is neglected in the simulations omitted [70]. It is worth mentioning that the thermal resistance between the cooling plates and the cell surface is neglected.

The next step after designing the 3D model is generating the mesh in COMOSL Multiphysics CFD software. The accuracy of the CFD model highly depends on the mesh elements. Also, the type of the defined solver is crucial to decrease the time of the simulation. Table 5
lists the grid independence analysis for the single-cell under the case studies of NC, PCM, and LCTMS; when the maximum number of mesh elements exceeds 21,789, 43,184, and 1,040,696 elements, respectively, the temperature difference is negligible. For the module of six LiC cells, when the maximum mesh elements exceed 128,119, 166,690, and 5,036,968 elements, respectively, the maximum temperature remains almost the same. Therefore, the mentioned maximum number of elements are selected to be used in the simulation analysis. The graphical mesh generation for the NC, PCM, and LCTMS case studies is exhibited in Fig. 12 for the single LiC cell and the module. The selected mesh elements are Mapped mesh and free quad elements in this study.

The accuracy of the simulations is checked by verifying the results against experiments. In this regard, all the initial and boundary conditions have been imported to the CFD tool in the heat transfer module and turbulent flow module of COMSOL Multiphysics®. The comparison study is conducted to verify the results in three case studies: natural convection (NC), PCM TMS, and liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS). Then, a module of six LiCs is designed to see the temperature rise of the module under NC. Moreover, the PCM and LCTMS thermal performances are checked in two separate models to investigate which system can optimally control the module's temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature evolution of the single LiC cell

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the simulation results of the LiC cell when it is charged/discharged with 150 A intense current rate without pause are explained. The heat generation equations for the NC case study should be solved thanks to the finite element method software, COMSOL Multiphysics®. Various parameters such as initial conditions, boundary conditions, design of the system, and thermal specifications affect the natural airflow. Fig. 13 shows the temperature distribution over the LiC cell, matching experimental data based on Fig. 16. The simulation error is 1% (0.6 °C) when is compared with the experiments. Due to the thermophysical specifications of the positive and negative tabs, the heat generation inside the LiC is non-uniform. Therefore, a robust cooling system is vital to distribute the temperature uniformly.

The temperature distribution of the LiC cell when pure paraffin PCM is employed as the TMS is illustrated in Fig. 14
. The simulation results are in good accordance with the experiments based on Fig. 16. The maximum temperature of the cell at the end of the test reaches 41.3 °C, in which the simulation error is 1.2% (0.5 °C). The temperature reduction in this method shows the PCM capability to absorb the generated heat during the cycling. However, to remove the absorbed heat from the PCM, another cooling system should be used parallel to enhance the PCM's performance and remove the absorbed heat to the environment (see Fig. 15).

The overall efficiency of the LCTMS is quite good due to its high thermal performance. The active LTMS reduces the maximum temperature of the cell below the safe limit at 32.9 °C. The CFD model of the LCTMS matches perfectly with the experiments based on Fig. 16. Although the thermal performance of this system is quite good, the temperature distribution for a module when LCTMS is the cooling system should be analyzed to conclude the robustness of LCTMS to be used in a module/pack.

Fig. 16 compares the experimental and simulation results at the end of the cycling tests. The experimental results are monitored by placing the thermocouple at the cell's hotspot, where the cell's temperature is almost maximum. The simulation temperature results are obtained by setting a probe in the CFD software while the thermocouple is mounted in the experimental test. The accordance of the experiments and simulation results is evident, for which the precision of the electro-thermal model utilized in the CFD simulations is proved. Moreover, the high thermal performance of the compact LCTMS compared with the other cooling techniques is observed.

Although the robustness of the LCTMS is evident for a single LiC cell, the thermal performance of the LCTMS and PCM should be checked for a module of six cells to investigate the effectiveness of these cooling methods for a module/pack design. To do so, the validated model of the single LiC cell is extended to six cells to make a module in the simulation analysis. Then, the temperature distribution along the module is investigated for the three case studies, including the NC, LCTMS, and PCM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature evolution of the module of six LiC cells

ce:section.ce:para: The single LiC cell is extended into a module of six cells to investigate its behavior under NC. As Fig. 17 illustrates, the module's temperature reaches 64.3 °C, which is way beyond the safe limit announced by the manufacturer. The maximum module temperature raised 9 °C compared to the single cell, mainly due to each cell's conduction effect on the adjacent cells. Moreover, only the first and the last cells are exposed to the heat flux from their left and right sides because there is no space between them.

From the uniformity viewpoint, it is evident that around 12 °C temperature difference is available that must be decreased to avoid hot temperature gradients inside the module in high power applications. The middle cells have the highest temperatures due to the conduction effect. By adding more space between the cells, the temperature of the middle cells would be diminished. Nevertheless, the maximum temperature would stay almost the same with little space between the cells. Therefore, the need for a proper TMS is appeared in this stage to remove the hotspots for extension of the module's lifetime, especially in high current rates.

The pure paraffin PCM added to the single-cell could decrease the maximum temperature by 26.2% (14.5 °C) compared to the NC case study. As Fig. 18 illustrates, the PCM container is extended in this section to study its performance for the module. Although the temperature of the LiC module was increased in the previous section, the extended PCM container can reduce the maximum temperature of the module by 24.5% (15.8 °C). However, the maximum temperature of the module is still beyond the safe operation range, which needs to be decreased more. As discussed in the previous sections, when PCMs work as the secondary cooling system, meaning that they help another active/passive cooling system absorb the generated heat from the cell/module, the system's overall performance would be enhanced.

Moreover, the hotspots in the middle of the module still remain, proving the non-uniformity of the PCM TMS. The temperature difference between the coldest and hottest cells is improved but is around 11 °C in the proposed passive cooling system for the module. Again, the fact that the thermal conductivity of the PCM cooling technique should be improved to release the absorbed generated heat to the environment by getting help from a secondary high thermal conductive medium. In the next chapter, a hybrid TMS will be proposed to minimize the temperature difference among the module and decrease the module's maximum temperature below the safe limit to allow the module to operate in high-power applications where fast charge/discharges are demanded.

In this section, the single LCTMS with two cold plates is extended to a module with six LiC cells and seven cold plates, meaning two cold plates for each cell. The maximum temperature of the single LiC between two cold plates was controlled at 32.5 °C a 41.2% improvement can be seen compared to the NC. For the module cooled by LCTMS, the performance is more or less the same (41.9%), and the LCTMS can keep the maximum temperature of the module in a desired range below the safe limit. Nonetheless, as shown in Fig. 19 , the temperature uniformity is around 9 °C, which should be further optimized.

In the LCTMS, the fluid flow removes the effect of conduction between each cell that caused to accumulation of hotspots in the middle of the module. However, since there is one inlet in the first cold plate and one outlet in the last cold plate, the fluid starts flowing from the first cold plate and paves the road among the other cold plates until it reaches the last cold plate exit the plates. Therefore, the inflow temperature is 23 °C while absorbing the heat from the cells through seven cold plates. At the last point, the outflow temperature reaches 36.1 °C causing accumulation of hotspots in the very last LiC cell. Hence, there is almost 7 °C temperature difference between the first and the last cell. This challenge can be solved using a passive TMS in parallel with the LCTMS.

In the previous chapters, some active and passive TMSs were investigated that PCM and LCTMS were selected due to their high performance to absorb and release the generated heat by the LiC cell/module. However, the fundamental problem inside a module is temperature non-uniformity that should be solved to avoid accumulating hotspots inside the module. In this regard, a hybrid TMS comprising the PCM and the LCTMS is proposed to decrease the maximum temperature and minimize the temperature difference between the cells. Fig. 20 demonstrates the overall architecture of the proposed hybrid system with the same thermo-physical specifications listed in Table 2.

The maximum temperature of the module is controlled at 32.1 °C, while the maximum temperature of the module under the NC case study was 64.3 °C. Therefore, the claim that the hybrid TMS has the highest performance with 50.1% enhancement compared to the module under NC is proved. The main advantage of hybridizing an active with a passive TMS is energy saving, where PCM requires no power source to operate. Besides, PCM will absorb the heat, and the LCTMS will help the PCM reject the ambient's absorbed heat. In the next chapter, the proposed hybrid system will be modeled and discussed in detail.

Furthermore, the most added value of the hybrid TMS is temperature uniformity along with the module. As Fig. 20 shows, the maximum temperature difference between the coldest and hottest point on the cells are 4.5 °C, which justifies the high thermal performance of this cooling technique.

Table 6 summarizes the numerical simulation results for the investigated TMSs for the single-cell and the module. As is evident, the hybrid TMS has the best overall performance to cool down the module and minimize the temperature difference to enhance the temperature uniformity and the cells.

In this work, a lithium-ion capacitor (LiC) was the target cell to investigate its thermal behavior in high power applications, where the cell is charged/discharged under 150 A intense current rate. Several active and passive TMSs were investigated, comprising an air-cooled TMS (ACTMS), and a compact liquid-cooled TMS (LCTMS) as active cooling systems. In addition, pure paraffin phase change material (PCM), PCM with added aluminum mesh grid foil (PCM-Al), PCM with an added heat sink (PCM-HS), and heat pipe cooling system (HPCS) were the examined passive cooling systems.

The experimental results showed that the LCTMS has the best performance among the proposed cooling systems. This system controls the maximum temperature at 32.5 °C (41.2%) compared to the NC with a maximum of 55.3 °C. Moreover, PCM-HS had the best performance among the passive systems by decreasing the maximum temperature from 55.3 °C to 34.1 °C with an overall temperature reduction of 38.3%. The high efficiency of the PCM-HS implies that the PCM can significantly absorb the generated heat during the test regime. Nonetheless, the PCM's deficiency to release the heat should be compensated by employing another system to remove the absorbed heat to the ambient. The experimental tests were conducted and verified against numerical analysis using a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software, COMSOL Multiphysics. After verifying the results, the single-cell was extended to a module of six cells to investigate how the cooling systems behave in higher power losses.

The investigated systems for making a module were NC, PCM, and LCTMS since they showed promising results to absorb the heat. In this regard, first, the simulation results for the single-cell under NC, the single-cell using the PCM as a cooling system, and the single-cell employing the LCTMS were verified against experiments. Then, the module of six cells was designed to study the thermal effectiveness of these selected systems. The simulation results exhibited that the module under NC experienced a high temperature around 64.3 °C, proving the need for a robust TMS for high power applications. The LCTMS and PCM can cool down the cells to 37.3 °C and 48.5 °C, respectively. Also, the temperature difference between the coldest and hottest points on the cell was high, around 9.2 °C and 11.3 °C for the LCTMS and PCM, respectively.

In the last part of this study, a hybrid topology comprising the LCTMS and the PCM was designed to decrease the maximum temperature and minimize the temperature difference among the cells. The simulation analysis demonstrated that the hybrid TMS can decrease the maximum temperature from 64.3 °C to 32.1 °C, meaning that a 50.1% decrease can be seen in the maximum temperature of the module. Moreover, the temperature uniformity of the module was enhanced significantly, showing a 4.5 °C difference between the hottest and coldest zones on the cell.

Danial Karimi: Conceptualization, methodology, software, visualization, validation, formal analysis, data Curation, investigation, writing – original draft, Hamidreza Behi: writing, review & Editing. Joeri Van Mierlo: supervision, review & editing, Maitane Berecibar: supervision, review & editing.
